coarse_alignment_reason: Whole-poem fallback because Chinese and English stanza structures do not align safely.
chinese_text:
鼓鍾將將，淮水湯湯。憂心且傷。淑人君子，懷允不忘。
鼓鍾喈喈，淮水湝湝。憂心且悲。淑人君子，其德不回。
鼓鍾伐鼛，淮有三洲。憂心且妯。淑人君子，其德不猶。
鼓鍾欽欽，鼓瑟鼓琴。笙磬同音。以雅以南，以籥不僭。
translation_text:
His bells ring out tseang-tseang,
While the waters of the Hwae go sweeping on;
Sad is my heart and wounded.
The virtuous sovereigns [of old], —
In my heart, indeed, I cannot forget them.

2 His drums ring out keae-lceae^
While the waters of the Hwae rush along;
My heart is sad and grieved.
Of the virtuous sovereigns [of old]
The virtue was without flaw.

3 His bells ring out, his large drums resound ;
There are the three islands in the Hwae;
Sad is my heart and moved.
Of the virtuous sovereigns [of old]
The virtue was different from this.

4 His bells ring out Vin-lcHn ;
His lutes, large and small, give their notes ;
The tones of his organs and sounding stones are in unison.
They sing the Ya aud the Nan,
Dancing to their flutes without error.

1 Thick grew the tribulus [on the ground],
But they cleared away its thorny bushes.
Why did they this of old?
That we might plant pur millet and sacrificial millet;
That our millet might be abundant,
And our sacrificial millet luxuriant.
When our barns are full,
